questions.original_question: What are the goals for this change of board members?
questions.answer:
The goals for the change of board members in Lotus Technology are primarily focused on enhancing governance, strategic oversight, and operational efficiency. The company aims to ensure that the board is composed of individuals with diverse expertise and experience, particularly in areas critical to Lotus’s growth, such as electrification, intelligentization, and international expansion. The board’s structure includes specialized committees like the Audit, Compensation, Nominating, and ESG committees, which are designed to provide focused oversight on key areas of the business. These changes are intended to support Lotus Technology’s mission of achieving sustainable growth, improving operational agility, and expanding its global footprint while maintaining high standards of corporate governance and transparency.

This information is based on knowledge available before 2024-05-10.

rag_info.sub_query: What are the goals for the change of board members in Lotus Technology?

rag_info.chunk_content:
• periodically reviewing and assessing the adequacy of the compensation committee charter and recommending any proposed changes to the board for approval; and   
• reviewing and assessing risks arising from Lotus Technology's employee compensation policies and practices and whether any such risks are reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on Lotus Technology. Nominating Committee. The nominating committee will consist of Shengyue Gui, Donghui Li, Stephen Brown Davis, and Michael David Ricks, and will be chaired by Shengyue Gui. Lotus Technology has determined that Stephen Brown Davis and Michael David Ricks satisfy the “independence” requirements of Rule 5605(a)(2) of the Listing Rules of the NYSE. The nominating committee assists the board of directors in selecting individuals qualified to become directors of Lotus Technology and in determining the composition of the board of directors and its committees. The nominating committee is responsible for, among other things: • overseeing searches for and identifying qualified individuals for membership on the board of directors and recommending individuals for membership on the board of directors and its committees for approval by the board of directors and/or the shareholders, if applicable; • at least annually leading the board of directors in a self-evaluation to determine whether it and its committees are functioning effectively, and reviewing the evaluations prepared by each board committee of such committee’s performance and considering any recommendations for proposed changes to the board of directors; • reviewing and approving compensation (including equity-based compensation) for Lotus Technology's directors;
rag_info.sub_query: What are the goals for the change of board members in Lotus Technology?
What are the goals for the change of board members in Lotus Technology?

rag_info.chunk_content: The board of directors of Lotus Technology will consist of eight directors, including four independent directors, namely Stephen Brown Davis, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks, upon the SEC’s declaration of effectiveness of Lotus Technology's registration statement on Form F-1 to which this prospectus forms a part. A director is not required to hold any shares in Lotus Technology to qualify to serve as a director. The Listing Rules of the NYSE generally require that a majority of an issuer’s board of directors must consist of independent directors. However, the Listing Rules of the NYSE permit foreign private issuers like Lotus Technology to follow “home country practice” in certain corporate governance matters. Lotus Technology relies on this “home country practice” exception and does not have a majority of independent directors serving on its board of directors. A director who is in any way, whether directly or indirectly, interested in a contract or proposed contract with Lotus Technology is required to declare the nature of his or her interest at a meeting of Lotus Technology's directors.
rag_info.sub_query: What are the goals for the change of board members in Lotus Technology?

rag_info.chunk_content: Lotus Technology intends to establish an audit committee, a compensation committee, a nominating committee, and an environmental, social, and corporate governance (“ESG”) committee under the board of directors immediately and adopt a charter for each of the four committees upon the effectiveness of the registration statement on Form F-1, of which this prospectus is a part. Lotus Technology intends to establish these committees prior to the completion of this offering. Each committee’s members and functions are described below. Audit Committee. The audit committee will consist of Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks, and is chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben. Lotus Technology has determined that each of Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks satisfies the “independence” requirements of Rule 5605(a)(2) of the Listing Rules of the NYSE and meets the independence standards under Rule 10A-3 under the Exchange Act. Lotus Technology has determined that Miguel A. Lopez Ben qualifies as an “audit committee financial expert.” The audit committee oversees Lotus Technology's accounting and financial reporting processes and the audits of the financial statements of Lotus Technology. The audit committee is responsible for, among other things: • the appointment, compensation, retention, termination, and oversight of the work of any accounting firm engaged for the purpose of preparing or issuing an audit report or performing other audit, review or attest services for Lotus Technology; • pre-approving the audit services and non-audit services to be provided by Lotus Technology's independent auditor;

metadata.title_summary:
title: Board of Directors 
summary: The company's board of directors will consist of eight members, including four independent directors, contingent upon the SEC's approval of the registration statement on Form F-1. While NYSE Listing Rules typically require a majority of independent directors, the company, as a foreign private issuer, will follow its home country practices, resulting in no majority of independent directors. Directors are not obligated to hold shares in the company and must disclose any direct or indirect interests in contracts during board meetings, with a general notice of affiliations being sufficient for voting on related transactions. They are permitted to vote on contracts in which they have an interest, and their votes will count towards the quorum. The board also has the authority to borrow money, mortgage assets, and issue securities as necessary. Notably, no director has a service contract that provides for benefits upon termination of their role.

The Listing Rules of the NYSE generally require that a majority of an issuer’s board of directors must consist of independent directors. However, the Listing Rules of the NYSE permit foreign private issuers like Lotus Technology to follow “home country practice” in certain corporate governance matters. Lotus Technology relies on this “home country practice” exception and does not have a majority of independent directors serving on its board of directors. A director who is in any way, whether directly or indirectly, interested in a contract or proposed contract with Lotus Technology is required to declare the nature of his or her interest at a meeting of Lotus Technology's board of directors.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Board of Directors 
summary: The board of directors will consist of eight members, including four independent directors: Stephen Brown Davis, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks, pending the SEC's approval of the registration statement on Form F-1 related to the prospectus. Directors are not required to hold shares in the company, and while NYSE Listing Rules usually necessitate a majority of independent directors, the company, as a foreign private issuer, will follow its home country practices, resulting in a board without a majority of independent members. Directors are required to disclose any direct or indirect interests in contracts during board meetings, with a general notice of affiliation sufficing for related transactions. They are permitted to vote on contracts in which they have an interest, and their votes will be counted in quorum calculations. The board also holds the authority to borrow funds, mortgage assets, and issue securities as necessary. Importantly, none of the directors have service contracts that include termination benefits.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Lotus Technology's board of directors consists of eight directors, including four independent directors, namely Stephen Brown Davis, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks. A director is not required to hold any shares in Lotus Technology to qualify to serve as a director. The Listing Rules of the NYSE generally require that a majority of an issuer’s board of directors must consist of independent directors. However, the Listing Rules of the NYSE permit foreign private issuers like Lotus Technology to follow “home country practice” in certain corporate governance matters. Lotus Technology relies on this “home country practice” exception and does not have a majority of independent directors serving on its board of directors. A director who is in any way, whether directly or indirectly, interested in a contract or proposed contract with Lotus Technology is required to declare the nature of his or her interest at a meeting of Lotus Technology's directors. A general notice given to the directors by any director to the effect that he or she is a member, shareholder, director, partner, officer, or employee of any specified company or firm and is to be regarded as interested in any contract or transaction with that company or firm shall be deemed a sufficient declaration of interest for the purposes of voting on a resolution in respect to a contract or transaction in which he/she has an interest, and after such general notice it shall not be necessary to give special notice relating to any particular transaction.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Board of Directors 
summary: The board of directors of our company consists of eight members, including four independent directors: Stephen Brown Davis, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks. While NYSE Listing Rules typically require a majority of independent directors, our status as a foreign private issuer allows us to follow our home country practices, resulting in a board that does not maintain this majority. Directors are not obligated to hold shares in the company, and they must disclose any interests in contracts during board meetings. A general notice of their affiliations with other companies is sufficient for voting on related matters, and they are permitted to vote on contracts in which they have an interest. The board has the authority to borrow funds, mortgage assets, and issue securities as necessary. Importantly, none of the directors have service contracts that provide benefits upon termination.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Board of directors 
summary: The company's board of directors consists of seven members, including three independent directors: Stephen Brown Davis, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, and Michael David Ricks. Unlike typical NYSE Listing Rules that require a majority of independent directors, foreign private issuers like this company can follow their home country practices, resulting in a board that does not have a majority of independent members. Directors are not obligated to hold shares in the company and must declare any direct or indirect interests in contracts during board meetings. A general notice of their association with a specific company suffices for voting on related transactions, allowing them to vote on contracts in which they have an interest, with their votes and presence counting towards quorum. The board also has the authority to borrow money, mortgage assets, and issue securities as necessary, and notably, none of the directors have service contracts that include termination benefits.

metadata.title_summary:
title: 2021 Share Incentive Plan 
summary: In August 2021, the board of directors approved the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, designed to attract and retain key personnel by aligning their interests with those of shareholders. The plan permits the issuance of up to 150 million ordinary shares for various equity awards, including restricted shares and restricted stock units (RSUs). As of December 31, 2024, 64,633,894 RSUs remain outstanding under this plan. 

The plan is managed by the chief executive officer or designated board members, who set the terms for each award, including exercise prices and vesting schedules. It includes provisions for transfer restrictions, allowing awards to be inherited but not sold or assigned, except back to the company. The plan can be modified by board resolution, but any changes cannot adversely affect participants' existing rights without their consent.

As of the end of 2024, the equity awards granted to directors and executive officers totaled 5,825,600 ordinary shares, all at an exercise price of $0.0002 per share, representing less than 1% of the company’s total outstanding shares. Notably, some of these awards replaced those from earlier incentive plans. Additionally, employees and other qualified individuals, excluding directors and executive officers, held 103,861,995 RSUs under the 2021 Plan. Overall, the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan serves as a strategic framework for fostering collaboration and achieving organizational goals while addressing potential challenges.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: The 2021 Plan may be altered in any respect by a resolution of the Board of Directors of Lotus Technology, provided that no such alteration shall operate to adversely affect any subsisting rights of any participant except with the consent in writing of the participant obtained amounting to three-fourths in nominal value of all awards granted and outstanding on that date. Term of the 2021 Plan. The 2021 Plan shall become effective when the Board of Directors of Lotus Technology approves the 2021 Plan, and shall be terminated by the Board of Directors of Lotus Technology, provided that such termination shall not affect any subsisting rights of any participant. The following table summarizes, as of the date of this prospectus, the number of ordinary shares under the equity awards that Lotus Technology granted to its directors and executive officers:

metadata.title_summary:
title: 2021 Share Incentive Plan 
summary: In August 2021, the board of directors approved the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, aimed at attracting and retaining key personnel while aligning their interests with those of shareholders. The plan allows for the issuance of up to 150,000,000 ordinary shares for various equity awards, including restricted shares and restricted stock units (RSUs). As of December 31, 2023, 93,011,731 RSUs have been granted and are outstanding under this plan. 

The plan is structured to ensure effective stakeholder engagement and resource allocation, with specific performance metrics and timelines for achieving its objectives. It is administered by the CEO or designated board members, who set the terms for each award, including vesting conditions and transfer restrictions. Notably, awards cannot be transferred except under specific circumstances, such as inheritance.

The plan can be modified by board resolution, but changes cannot adversely affect participants' existing rights without their consent. The plan remains effective until terminated by the board, although accrued rights prior to termination are preserved. A summary table details equity awards granted to directors and executive officers, revealing that many did not receive awards, while a select few were granted a total of 5,600,000 ordinary shares. Additionally, the report references accounting policies related to share-based compensation for further details.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The organization is establishing four committees under its board of directors: an Audit Committee, a Compensation Committee, a Nominating Committee, and an Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance (ESG) Committee. These committees will be formed immediately upon the effectiveness of their registration statement on Form F-1, prior to the completion of the offering, with specific member details and functions to be outlined in accompanying documentation.

The Audit Committee, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben and comprising members who meet NYSE independence criteria, is tasked with overseeing the company's financial reporting and auditing processes. Its key responsibilities include engaging and overseeing accounting firms, pre-approving audit services, conducting annual performance evaluations of auditors, overseeing internal audit functions, reviewing financial reports, discussing significant financial issues, assessing internal controls, and managing related party transactions.

The Compensation Committee, led by Donghui Li, focuses on reviewing and approving compensation structures for directors and executive officers, excluding the CEO from discussions about their compensation. Its responsibilities encompass evaluating executive compensation packages, management succession planning, and assessing risks associated with compensation policies.

The Nominating Committee, chaired by Shengyue Gui, assists the board in identifying qualified candidates for directorship and shaping board composition. Its duties include recommending board members, conducting self-evaluations, reviewing director compensation, overseeing director development, and evaluating its own performance.

The ESG Committee, chaired by Conghui An, supports the board in overseeing ESG-related matters. Its responsibilities involve assessing current and emerging ESG issues, analyzing associated risks and opportunities, developing strategies and policies, setting and monitoring ESG goals, and integrating ESG considerations into business operations. This structured approach aims to enhance the company's resilience and reputation while promoting sustainable growth through effective ESG practices.

Lotus Technology intends to establish these committees prior to the completion of this offering. Each committee’s members and functions are described below. Audit Committee. The audit committee will consist of Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks, and is chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben. Lotus Technology has determined that each of Miguel A. Lopez Ben, Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks satisfies the “independence” requirements of Rule 5605(a)(2) of the Listing Rules of the NYSE and meets the independence standards under Rule 10A-3 under the Exchange Act. Lotus Technology has determined that Miguel A. Lopez Ben qualifies as an “audit committee financial expert.” The audit committee oversees Lotus Technology's accounting and financial reporting processes and the audits of the financial statements of Lotus Technology. The audit committee is responsible for, among other things: • the appointment, compensation, retention, termination, and oversight of the work of any accounting firm engaged for the purpose of preparing or issuing an audit report or performing other audit, review or attest services for Lotus Technology; • pre-approving the audit services and non-audit services to be provided by Lotus Technology's independent auditor;

metadata.title_summary:
title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The company is establishing four committees under its board of directors: the Audit Committee, Compensation Committee, Nominating Committee, and Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance (ESG) Committee. These committees will be formed immediately following the effectiveness of their registration statement on Form F-1, with details on their members and functions included in the accompanying documentation.

The Audit Committee, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben and comprising members who meet independence requirements, is responsible for overseeing the company's financial reporting processes and audits. Its key duties include engaging and overseeing external auditors, pre-approving audit services, conducting annual evaluations of auditor performance, reviewing financial statements, and assessing internal controls and related party transactions.

The Compensation Committee, led by Donghui Li, focuses on reviewing and approving compensation structures for directors and executive officers, excluding the CEO from discussions about their compensation. It evaluates management succession planning, assesses compensation policies for potential risks, and regularly reviews its charter for adequacy.

The Nominating Committee, chaired by Shengyue Gui, assists in identifying qualified candidates for board positions and shaping board composition. Its responsibilities include conducting annual evaluations of board effectiveness, reviewing director compensation, and overseeing director development programs.

The ESG Committee, chaired by Conghui An, addresses critical environmental, social, and governance issues relevant to the company's operations. It evaluates current and emerging ESG matters, analyzes risks and opportunities, develops strategies and policies, sets goals for ESG initiatives, and ensures the integration of ESG practices into daily operations.

Together, these committees are designed to enhance corporate governance, ensure compliance with regulations, and promote sustainability and ethical practices within the organization.

title: OUR GROWTH STRATEGIES 
summary: The company is set to implement a range of growth strategies designed to strengthen its market leadership and ensure sustainable success. These initiatives will focus on enhancing competitive advantages, expanding market reach, and fostering innovation to adapt to changing customer needs. Key areas of commitment include increased investment in research and development, particularly in electrification and intelligent technologies, as well as the introduction of new models to diversify the product portfolio. The company also plans to broaden its international presence, improve agile development capabilities, and boost operational efficiency. Additionally, efforts will be made to expand the sales and service network and enhance customer engagement, all while prioritizing an improved customer experience through comprehensive lifecycle services. Overall, these strategies aim to enhance performance and drive long-term success for the organization.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Technological innovation is critical to Lotus Technology's success, and Lotus Technology strategically develops most of its key technologies in-house, such as its electrical and electronic architecture, or E/E Architecture, ZEEKR OS, Firmware Over-the-Air, or FOTA, and electric powertrain. Lotus Technology has been investing heavily in its research and development efforts. The BEV industry is experiencing rapid changes in technology, and Lotus Technology needs to invest significant resources in research and development to lead technological breakthroughs in order to remain competitive in the market. Therefore, Lotus Technology expects that its research and development expenses will continue to be significant. Furthermore, research and development activities are inherently uncertain, and there can be no assurance that Lotus Technology will continue to achieve technological breakthroughs and successfully commercialize such breakthroughs. As a result, Lotus Technology's significant expenditures on research and development may not generate corresponding benefits. If Lotus Technology's research and development efforts fail to keep up with the latest technological developments, it would suffer a decline in its competitive position. If Lotus Technology made any misjudgment with respect to technological developments or experiences any delay or other setbacks in its efforts to improve its technology, it could materially and adversely affect its business, results of operations, and prospects. In addition, Lotus Technology's revenue generated from research and development services and other services depends on varied customer needs and specific delivery timelines in its customers’ ordinary course of business.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Our research and development efforts may not yield expected results. 
summary: The company recognizes that technological innovation is vital for its success, prioritizing the in-house development of essential technologies such as electrical and electronic architecture, ZEEKR OS, Firmware Over-the-Air (FOTA), and electric powertrains. To remain competitive in the rapidly evolving battery electric vehicle (BEV) industry, substantial investments in research and development (R&D) are necessary. However, these R&D efforts come with inherent uncertainties, as there is no assurance that such investments will lead to successful technological advancements or commercial viability. Failing to keep up with technological progress could jeopardize the company's competitive standing. Furthermore, the revenue generated from R&D services is subject to fluctuations based on customer demands and project timelines, with a notable decline in project acceptances anticipated in early 2024 compared to late 2023, which is expected to negatively impact revenue and gross profit.

The company also relies on both its own expertise and external supplier technologies, particularly in areas like battery cells and semiconductors, which it does not produce. This reliance introduces uncertainty regarding the integration of the latest technologies into its BEVs. As technology continues to advance, the company intends to upgrade existing models and launch new ones, which may involve significant costs and could result in lower returns on investment for current models. Additionally, even if the company successfully navigates technological changes, older models may become obsolete more quickly than expected, further affecting overall return on investment.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: For example, the number of research and development projects that reached customer acceptance and payment stage in the first quarter of 2024 was less than that of the fourth quarter of 2023, which is expected to cause a significant quarter-over-quarter decrease in revenue generated from research and development services and other services as well as the gross profit in relation to Lotus Technology's research and development services and other services in the first quarter of 2024. Besides Lotus Technology's in-house expertise, Lotus Technology also relies on certain technologies of its suppliers to enhance the performance of its battery electric vehicles (BEVs). In particular, Lotus Technology does not manufacture battery cells or semiconductors, which makes the company dependent upon suppliers for the relevant technologies. There can be no assurance that Lotus Technology will be able to equip its BEVs with the latest technologies. As technologies change, Lotus Technology plans to upgrade its existing models and introduce new models in order to provide its BEVs with the latest technologies, including battery cells and semiconductors, which could involve substantial costs and lower the return on investment for existing models. Even if Lotus Technology is able to keep pace with changes in technologies and develop new models, its prior models could become obsolete more quickly than expected, potentially reducing the return on investment.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Basis for Opinion 
summary: The financial statements of the Company are the responsibility of its management, while the role of Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu Certified Public Accountants LLP, a PCAOB-registered public accounting firm, is to provide an independent audit opinion. The audits were conducted in accordance with PCAOB standards to ensure reasonable assurance that the financial statements are free from material misstatements due to error or fraud. Although the Company is not required to undergo an audit of its internal controls, the auditors gained an understanding of these systems without expressing an opinion on their effectiveness. The audit process involved assessing risks, testing evidence related to financial statement amounts, and evaluating management's accounting principles and estimates.

Deloitte, a global leader in professional services, has served as the Company’s auditor since 2021. The audited financial statements include a detailed balance sheet for ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited, showing significant growth in assets from 2021 to 2023. Key highlights include a decrease in cash and cash equivalents, a substantial increase in restricted cash, and notable growth in accounts receivable and inventories. Total assets rose from approximately 11.94 billion RMB in 2021 to 27.12 billion RMB in 2023, indicating a strong financial position.

Additionally, Hangzhou, the capital of Zhejiang Province in China, is highlighted for its rich cultural heritage and economic significance, particularly as the headquarters of Alibaba Group. The city is renowned for its scenic landscapes, historical sites, and vibrant economy, blending modern development with traditional charm. The accompanying notes in the financial statements provide crucial context and disclosures, enhancing the understanding of the Company's financial performance and position.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: These financial statements are the responsibility of Lotus Technology’s management. The responsibility of the public accounting firm is to express an opinion on Lotus Technology’s financial statements based on the audits. The public accounting firm is registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (PCAOB) and is required to be independent with respect to Lotus Technology in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB. Lotus Technology's management conducted its audits in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that Lotus Technology's management plans and performs the audit to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud. Lotus Technology is not required to have, nor was the public accounting firm engaged to perform, an audit of its internal control over financial reporting. As part of the audits, the public accounting firm is required to obtain an understanding of internal control over financial reporting but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of Lotus Technology’s internal control over financial reporting. Accordingly, the public accounting firm expresses no such opinion. Lotus Technology's audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the financial statements.

all_retrieved_content.page_content:
[Table Level]
- Table Title: ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited Combined and Consolidated Balance Sheets
- Table Summary: This table provides a detailed breakdown of the assets held by ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited as of December 31 for the years 2021, 2022, and 2023. The values are presented in thousands of RMB and converted to USD according to Note 2(d).
- Context: The table is part of the audited financial statements of ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited, providing an overview of the company’s asset composition and financial health over three years, as examined by Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu.
- Special Notes: The values are stated in RMB, with equivalent values in USD stated according to Note 2(d). Specific allowances for doubtful accounts are mentioned alongside accounts receivable and related parties. Amount in thousands.

[Row Level]
Row 1: As of December 31, 2021, ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited held cash and cash equivalents totaling 3,893,980 RMB, which increased to 3,561,544 RMB in 2022 and then slightly decreased to 3,260,670 RMB in 2023, equivalent to 459,256 USD.
Row 2: Restricted cash was reported at 3,986 RMB in 2021, increased to 193,360 RMB in 2022, and amounted to 844,079 RMB in 2023, translating to 118,886 USD.
Row 3: Notes receivable were valued at 33,881 RMB in 2021, grew to 148,673 RMB in 2022, and further increased to 487,851 RMB in 2023, which is 68,712 USD.
Row 4: Accounts receivable, net of specific allowances for doubtful accounts, were 24,208 RMB in 2021, 158,581 RMB in 2022, and reached 1,104,450 RMB in 2023, equal to 155,559 USD.
Row 5: Inventories were valued at 1,214,080 RMB at the end of 2021, increased significantly to 3,164,809 RMB in 2022, and peaked at 5,228,689 RMB in 2023, which represents 736,445 USD.
Row 6: Amounts due from related parties, factoring in specific allowances for doubtful accounts, were 3,848,577 RMB in 2021, grew to 6,132,982 RMB in 2022, and climbed to 7,256,861 RMB by 2023, equivalent to 1,022,107 USD.
Row 7: Prepayments and other current assets, also net of specified allowances for doubtful accounts, were reported at 413,095 RMB in 2021, rose to 1,240,175 RMB in 2022, and increased to 2,294,508 RMB in 2023, translating to 323,175 USD.
Row 8: The total current assets stood at 9,431,807 RMB in 2021, increased substantially to 14,600,124 RMB in 2022, and further to 20,477,108 RMB in 2023, which is 2,884,140 USD.
Row 9: Property, plant, and equipment, net of depreciation, were valued at 1,092,759 RMB in 2021, increased to 1,953,846 RMB in 2022, and reached 2,914,274 RMB in 2023, equaling 410,467 USD.
Row 10: Intangible assets, net, were recorded at 36,396 RMB in 2021, increased significantly to 109,947 RMB in 2022, and amounted to 410,912 RMB by 2023, equal to 57,876 USD.
Row 11: Land use rights, net, were valued at 54,108 RMB at the end of 2021, slightly increased to 52,932 RMB in 2022, and decreased to 51,755 RMB in 2023, translating to 7,290 USD.
Row 12: Operating lease right-of-use assets were reported at 1,000,052 RMB in 2021, increased to 2,077,072 RMB in 2022, and were 2,443,545 RMB in 2023, which represents 344,166 USD.
Row 13: Deferred tax assets were 75,395 RMB in 2021, increased to 46,888 RMB in 2022, and amounted to 86,395 RMB in 2023, equal to 12,168 USD.
Row 14: Long-term investments were valued at 192,221 RMB in 2021, increased to 372,952 RMB in 2022, and were 459,794 RMB in 2023, equating to 64,761 USD.
Row 15: Other non-current assets stood at 57,194 RMB in 2021, increased to 263,555 RMB in 2022, and were equal to 273,717 RMB in 2023, translating to 38,552 USD.
Row 16: The total non-current assets were reported at 2,508,125 RMB in 2021, grew to 4,877,192 RMB in 2022, and reached 6,640,392 RMB by 2023, which is 935,280 USD.
Row 17: Total assets were valued at 11,939,932 RMB at the end of 2021, increased significantly to 19,477,316 RMB in 2022, and reached 27,117,500 RMB in 2023, equivalent to 3,819,420 USD.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Technological innovation is critical to Lotus Technology's success, and Lotus Technology strategically develops most of its key technologies in-house, such as its electrical and electronic architecture, or E/E Architecture, Zeekr OS, Firmware Over-the-Air, or FOTA, and electric powertrain. Lotus Technology has been investing heavily in its research and development efforts. The BEV industry is experiencing rapid changes in technology, and Lotus Technology needs to invest significant resources in research and development to lead technological breakthroughs in order to remain competitive in the market. Therefore, Lotus Technology expects that its research and development expenses will continue to be significant. Furthermore, research and development activities are inherently uncertain, and there can be no assurance that Lotus Technology will continue to achieve technological breakthroughs and successfully commercialize such breakthroughs. As a result, Lotus Technology's significant expenditures on research and development may not generate corresponding benefits. If Lotus Technology's research and development efforts fail to keep up with the latest technological developments, it would suffer a decline in its competitive position. If Lotus Technology made any misjudgment with respect to technological developments or experienced any delay or other setbacks in its efforts to improve its technology, it could materially and adversely affect its business, results of operations, and prospects. In addition, Lotus Technology's revenue generated from research and development services and other services depends on varied customer needs and specific delivery timelines in its customers’ ordinary course of business.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Our research and development efforts may not yield expected results. 
summary: The company recognizes technological innovation as a cornerstone of its success, prioritizing the in-house development of essential technologies such as electrical and electronic architecture, Zeekr OS, Firmware Over-the-Air (FOTA), and electric powertrains. To thrive in the fast-paced battery electric vehicle (BEV) market, substantial investments in research and development (R&D) are essential. However, the unpredictable nature of R&D presents risks, as there are no assurances of achieving significant technological advancements or successfully bringing them to market, which could result in considerable costs without adequate returns. Additionally, the company’s reliance on supplier technologies for battery performance, due to the absence of in-house battery cell and semiconductor manufacturing, raises concerns about integrating the latest innovations into their vehicles. While plans to upgrade existing models and launch new ones aim to keep pace with technological changes, these initiatives may lead to high expenses and affect the return on investment for current models. Furthermore, the risk of older models becoming obsolete more quickly than expected could further undermine profitability, complicating the company’s operational landscape.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Besides Lotus Technology's in-house expertise, Lotus Technology also relies on certain technologies of its suppliers to enhance the performance of its battery electric vehicles (BEVs). In particular, Lotus Technology does not manufacture battery cells or semiconductors, which makes Lotus Technology dependent upon suppliers for the relevant technologies. There can be no assurance that Lotus Technology will be able to equip its BEVs with the latest technologies. As technologies change, Lotus Technology plans to upgrade its existing models and introduce new models in order to provide its BEVs with the latest technologies, including battery cells and semiconductors, which could involve substantial costs and lower Lotus Technology's return on investment for existing models. Even if Lotus Technology is able to keep pace with changes in technologies and develop new models, its prior models could become obsolete more quickly than expected, potentially reducing Lotus Technology's return on investment.

metadata.title_summary:
title: Our research and development efforts may not yield expected results. 
summary: The company recognizes that technological innovation is crucial for its success, prioritizing the in-house development of essential technologies such as electrical and electronic architecture, ZEEKR OS, Firmware Over-the-Air (FOTA), and electric powertrains. To remain competitive in the fast-paced Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) market, substantial investments in research and development (R&D) are necessary. However, the unpredictable nature of R&D means that these investments may not always yield expected benefits, and failing to keep up with technological advancements could jeopardize the company's competitive edge. Moreover, revenue from R&D services is influenced by customer demands and timelines, leading to a decrease in projects progressing to acceptance and payment stages in early 2024 compared to late 2023, which is anticipated to significantly lower revenue and gross profit from these services in the first quarter of 2024. The company also relies on external suppliers for critical components like battery cells and semiconductors, which introduces uncertainty in equipping its BEVs with the latest technologies. To enhance competitiveness, the company plans to upgrade existing models and launch new ones, although this may involve considerable costs and affect the return on investment for current models. Additionally, there is a risk that older models could become obsolete more quickly than expected, further impacting potential returns.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Lotus Technology notes your response to its prior comment 3 and reissues in part. Please revise the risk factors to discuss the arbitration provision's related risks to investors, such as increased costs to bring a claim, limited access to information, and other imbalances of resources between the company and shareholders. Lotus Technology respectfully advises the Staff that Lotus Technology has revised the disclosure on pages 75 to 76 in the Revised Registration Statement in response to the Staff’s comment. 2. Lotus Technology requests that the compensation disclosure be updated to reflect the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023. Lotus Technology respectfully advises the Staff that Lotus Technology has updated the disclosure on page 185 in the Revised Registration Statement in response to the Staff’s comment. If you have any questions regarding this submission, please contact Ran Li at +86-10-8567-5013 (ran.li@davispolk.com). Thank you again for your time and attention. Yours sincerely,

metadata.title_summary:
title: General 
summary: The company has acknowledged the previous comments and is requesting a partial reissuance of its documentation. It has been advised to revise its risk factors to include a discussion on the risks related to the arbitration provision, highlighting potential increased costs for investors, limited access to information, and the resource disparities between the company and its shareholders. In response to the Staff's comments, the company has updated its disclosures on pages 75 to 76 and page 185 of the Revised Registration Statement, including a more accurate representation of financial information for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2023. For any inquiries regarding this submission, please contact Ran Li at +86-10-8567-5013 or via email at ran.li@davispolk.com. Thank you for your attention.

• reviewing Lotus Technology's disclosure controls and procedures and internal control over financial reporting, policies and practices with respect to risk assessment and risk management; • reviewing and assessing the adequacy of the audit committee charter periodically and recommending any proposed changes to the board; and   
• reviewing and approving all related party transactions. Compensation Committee. The compensation committee will consist of Donghui Li, Conghui An, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, and Michael David Ricks and is chaired by Donghui Li. Lotus Technology has determined that Miguel A. Lopez Ben and Michael David Ricks satisfy the “independence” requirements of Rule 5605(a)(2) of the Listing Rules of the NYSE. The compensation committee assists the board of directors in reviewing and approving the compensation structure, including all forms of compensation, relating to Lotus Technology's directors and executive officers. Lotus Technology's chief executive officer may not be present at any committee meeting during which their compensation is deliberated upon. The compensation committee is responsible for, among other things: • reviewing and approving the compensation of each of Lotus Technology's executive officers; • periodically reviewing Lotus Technology's management succession planning, including policies for executive officers’ selection and succession in the event of the incapacitation, retirement or removal of the executive officers, and evaluations of, and development plans for, any potential successors to the executive officers; • reviewing and submitting for the board's approval Lotus Technology's executive compensation and benefits policies generally;

metadata.title_summary:
title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The company is establishing four committees under its board of directors: the Audit Committee, Compensation Committee, Nominating Committee, and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Committee, effective upon the registration statement on Form F-1. Each committee will be formed prior to the completion of the offering, with specific details about their members and functions outlined in accompanying documentation.

The **Audit Committee**, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben and comprising members who meet NYSE independence criteria, will oversee the company's financial reporting and audit processes. Key responsibilities include engaging and overseeing accounting firms, conducting annual reviews of auditor performance, evaluating the internal audit function, approving financial reports, and assessing internal controls and related party transactions to ensure compliance and integrity in financial reporting.

The **Compensation Committee**, led by Donghui Li, will focus on reviewing and approving compensation structures for directors and executive officers, excluding the CEO from discussions about their own compensation. This committee will also assess management succession planning, evaluate compensation policies, and identify any risks associated with employee compensation practices.

The **Nominating Committee**, chaired by Shengyue Gui, will assist the board in identifying qualified candidates for directorship and shaping the board's composition. Responsibilities include recommending candidates for board and committee membership, leading self-evaluations of the board's effectiveness, reviewing director compensation, and overseeing educational programs for board members.

The **ESG Committee**, chaired by Conghui An, will support the board in managing ESG-related issues. Its responsibilities encompass assessing current and emerging ESG matters, analyzing associated risks and opportunities, developing strategies and policies aligned with ESG objectives, setting and monitoring specific goals, and integrating ESG practices into daily operations. The committee will also oversee transparency in ESG reporting and communications with stakeholders.

Together, these committees are designed to enhance governance, ensure compliance, and promote sustainable practices within the company.

all_retrieved_content.page_content:
• periodically reviewing and assessing the adequacy of the compensation committee charter and recommending any proposed changes to the board for approval; and   
• reviewing and assessing risks arising from Lotus Technology's employee compensation policies and practices and whether any such risks are reasonably likely to have a material adverse effect on Lotus Technology. Nominating Committee. The nominating committee will consist of Shengyue Gui, Donghui Li, Stephen Brown Davis, and Michael David Ricks, and will be chaired by Shengyue Gui. Lotus Technology has determined that Stephen Brown Davis and Michael David Ricks satisfy the “independence” requirements of Rule 5605(a)(2) of the Listing Rules of the NYSE. The nominating committee assists the board of directors in selecting individuals qualified to become directors of Lotus Technology and in determining the composition of the board of directors and its committees. The nominating committee is responsible for, among other things: • overseeing searches for and identifying qualified individuals for membership on the board of directors and recommending individuals for membership on the board of directors and its committees for approval by the board of directors and/or the shareholders, if applicable; • at least annually leading the board of directors in a self-evaluation to determine whether it and its committees are functioning effectively, and reviewing the evaluations prepared by each board committee of such committee’s performance and considering any recommendations for proposed changes to the board of directors; • reviewing and approving compensation (including equity-based compensation) for Lotus Technology's directors;

title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The organization is establishing four committees under its board of directors: an Audit Committee, a Compensation Committee, a Nominating Committee, and an Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance (ESG) Committee. These committees will be formed immediately upon the effectiveness of their registration statement on Form F-1, prior to the completion of the offering, with specific member details and functions to be outlined in accompanying documentation.

The Audit Committee, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben and comprising members who meet NYSE independence criteria, is tasked with overseeing the company's financial reporting and auditing processes. Its key responsibilities include engaging and overseeing accounting firms, pre-approving audit services, conducting annual performance evaluations of auditors, overseeing internal audit functions, reviewing financial reports, discussing significant financial issues, assessing internal controls, and managing related party transactions.

The Compensation Committee, led by Donghui Li, focuses on reviewing and approving compensation structures for directors and executive officers, excluding the CEO from discussions about their compensation. Its responsibilities encompass evaluating executive compensation packages, management succession planning, and assessing risks associated with compensation policies.

The Nominating Committee, chaired by Shengyue Gui, assists the board in identifying qualified candidates for directorship and shaping board composition. Its duties include recommending board members, conducting self-evaluations, reviewing director compensation, overseeing director development, and evaluating its own performance.

The ESG Committee, chaired by Conghui An, supports the board in overseeing ESG-related matters. Its responsibilities involve assessing current and emerging ESG issues, analyzing associated risks and opportunities, developing strategies and policies, setting and monitoring ESG goals, and integrating ESG considerations into business operations. This structured approach aims to enhance the company's resilience and reputation while promoting sustainable growth through effective ESG practices.

title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The company is establishing four committees under its board of directors: the Audit Committee, Compensation Committee, Nominating Committee, and Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance (ESG) Committee. These committees will be formed immediately following the effectiveness of their registration statement on Form F-1, with details on their members and functions included in the accompanying documentation.

The Audit Committee, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben and comprising members who meet independence requirements, is responsible for overseeing the company's financial reporting processes and audits. Its key duties include engaging and overseeing external auditors, pre-approving audit services, conducting annual evaluations of auditor performance, reviewing financial statements, and assessing internal controls and related party transactions.

The Compensation Committee, led by Donghui Li, focuses on reviewing and approving compensation structures for directors and executive officers, excluding the CEO from discussions about their compensation. It evaluates management succession planning, assesses compensation policies for potential risks, and regularly reviews its charter for adequacy.

The Nominating Committee, chaired by Shengyue Gui, assists in identifying qualified candidates for board positions and shaping board composition. Its responsibilities include conducting annual evaluations of board effectiveness, reviewing director compensation, and overseeing director development programs.

The ESG Committee, chaired by Conghui An, addresses critical environmental, social, and governance issues relevant to the company's operations. It evaluates current and emerging ESG matters, analyzes risks and opportunities, develops strategies and policies, sets goals for ESG initiatives, and ensures the integration of ESG practices into daily operations.

Together, these committees are designed to enhance corporate governance, ensure compliance with regulations, and promote sustainability and ethical practices within the organization.

title: Committees of the Board of Directors 
summary: The organization has established four key committees under its board of directors: the Audit Committee, Compensation Committee, Nominating Committee, and Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Committee, each with its own charter and defined responsibilities.

The **Audit Committee**, chaired by Miguel A. Lopez Ben, includes members Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks. This committee ensures the integrity of financial reporting and compliance with regulations. Its primary responsibilities encompass overseeing the engagement and performance of external auditors, approving audit and non-audit services, evaluating the internal audit function, and reviewing financial statements and related party transactions.

The **Compensation Committee**, led by Donghui Li and comprising Conghui An, Miguel A. Lopez Ben, and Michael David Ricks, focuses on executive compensation and succession planning. This committee evaluates compensation packages for executives, reviews management succession plans, and recommends policies to ensure competitive and equitable compensation practices.

The **Nominating Committee**, chaired by Shengyue Gui and including Donghui Li, Stephen Brown Davis, and Michael David Ricks, is tasked with identifying qualified candidates for board membership and assessing the board's composition. It conducts annual evaluations of board effectiveness, manages director orientation and education, and reviews compensation for directors.

Lastly, the **ESG Committee**, led by Conghui An and including Stephen Brown Davis, Latha Maripuri, and Michael David Ricks, oversees the company's ESG initiatives. Its responsibilities include identifying and assessing ESG risks and opportunities, developing strategies and policies, setting goals for ESG performance, and ensuring effective communication with stakeholders regarding ESG matters.

Together, these committees play a crucial role in governance, risk management, and the promotion of ethical practices within the organization, contributing to its long-term success and reputation as a responsible corporate entity.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Lotus Technology built an R&D team with extensive industry knowledge and experience. Over the years, Lotus Technology has recruited R&D talents from Geely Group as well as other established technology companies. The R&D team consists of members who are professional and recognize Lotus Technology's corporate goals. Lotus Technology has developed three designated teams focused on vehicle-mounted technologies and six R&D centers dedicated to autonomous driving, ZEEKR OS, E/E Architecture, software, and internet.

Other than what has been included in the capital commitments and contractual obligations discussed below, Lotus Technology does not expect to incur material costs to accomplish these goals.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Shareholders of Cayman Islands exempted companies like Lotus Technology have no general rights under Cayman Islands law to inspect corporate records or to obtain copies of lists of shareholders of these companies. The directors of Lotus Technology will have discretion under Lotus Technology's memorandum and articles of association to determine whether or not, and under what conditions, Lotus Technology's corporate records may be inspected by its shareholders, but are not obliged to make them available to Lotus Technology's shareholders. This may make it more difficult for shareholders to obtain the information needed to establish any facts necessary for a shareholder resolution or to solicit proxies from other shareholders in connection with a proxy contest. As a result of all of the above, Lotus Technology's public shareholders may have more difficulty in protecting their interests in the face of actions taken by Lotus Technology's management, members of the board of directors, or controlling shareholders than they would as public shareholders of a company incorporated in the United States.

metadata.title_summary:
title: You may face difficulties in protecting your interests, and your ability to protect your rights through U.S. courts may be limited, because we are incorporated under Cayman Islands law. 
summary: The company operates as an exempted entity limited by shares under Cayman Islands law, with its governance dictated by its memorandum and articles of association, the revised Companies Act, and applicable common law. Shareholder rights, minority actions, and directors' fiduciary duties are primarily shaped by common law, which is influenced by English legal precedents but lacks binding authority in the Cayman Islands. In contrast to jurisdictions like Delaware in the U.S., the legal framework for shareholder rights and director responsibilities in the Cayman Islands is less defined and lacks comprehensive securities laws. Consequently, Cayman Islands companies may not have the legal standing to initiate shareholder derivative actions in U.S. federal courts, limiting their ability to pursue claims on behalf of shareholders. Additionally, shareholders do not possess general rights to inspect corporate records or access shareholder lists, as such decisions are at the discretion of the directors. This restriction may impede shareholders' efforts to obtain information for resolutions or proxy solicitations, resulting in greater challenges for public shareholders in protecting their interests against management or controlling shareholders compared to their U.S. counterparts.

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Shareholders of Cayman Islands exempted companies like Lotus Technology have no general rights under Cayman Islands law to inspect corporate records or to obtain copies of lists of shareholders of these companies. The directors of Lotus Technology will have discretion under the third amended and restated memorandum and articles of association, which are both expected to be effective immediately prior to completion of this offering, to determine whether or not, and under what conditions, the corporate records may be inspected by the shareholders, but are not obliged to make them available to the shareholders. This may make it more difficult for shareholders to obtain the information needed to establish any facts necessary for a shareholder resolution or to solicit proxies from other shareholders in connection with a proxy contest. As a result of all of the above, Lotus Technology's public shareholders may have more difficulty in protecting their interests in the face of actions taken by Lotus Technology's management, members of the board of directors, or controlling shareholders than they would as public shareholders of a company incorporated in the United States. For a discussion of significant differences between the provisions of the Companies Act (as revised) of the Cayman Islands and the laws applicable to companies incorporated in the United States and their shareholders, see “Description of Share Capital — Differences in Corporate Law.”

metadata.title_summary:
title: You may face difficulties in protecting your interests, and your ability to protect your rights through U.S. courts may be limited, because we are incorporated under Cayman Islands law. 
summary: The company is an exempted entity limited by shares under Cayman Islands law, governed by its memorandum and articles of association, the revised Companies Act, and applicable common law. Shareholder rights and directors' fiduciary duties are primarily dictated by common law, which is shaped by limited local precedents and influenced by English common law, although English court rulings are merely persuasive. In contrast to jurisdictions like Delaware in the U.S., the legal framework in the Cayman Islands is less defined, particularly concerning securities laws and corporate governance. Notably, shareholders of Cayman Islands companies do not possess general rights to inspect corporate records or obtain shareholder lists, as access is at the discretion of the directors. This limitation may impede shareholders' ability to gather essential information for decision-making or proxy contests. Consequently, public shareholders may encounter more significant challenges in protecting their interests against management, the board, or controlling shareholders compared to their U.S. counterparts. The differences in corporate governance and shareholder protections between the Cayman Islands and U.S. laws are further elaborated in the section titled "Description of Share Capital — Differences in Corporate Law."

all_retrieved_content.page_content: Shareholders of Cayman Islands exempted companies like Lotus Technology have no general rights under Cayman Islands law to inspect corporate records or to obtain copies of lists of shareholders of these companies. The directors of Lotus Technology will have discretion under the third amended and restated memorandum and articles of association, which are both expected to be effective immediately prior to completion of this offering, to determine whether or not, and under what conditions, the corporate records of Lotus Technology may be inspected by its shareholders, but are not obliged to make them available to its shareholders. This may make it more difficult for shareholders to obtain the information needed to establish any facts necessary for a shareholder resolution or to solicit proxies from other shareholders in connection with a proxy contest. As a result of all of the above, Lotus Technology's public shareholders may have more difficulty in protecting their interests in the face of actions taken by Lotus Technology's management, members of the board of directors, or controlling shareholders than they would as public shareholders of a company incorporated in the United States. For a discussion of significant differences between the provisions of the Companies Act (as revised) of the Cayman Islands and the laws applicable to companies incorporated in the United States and their shareholders, see “Description of Share Capital — Differences in Corporate Law.”

metadata.title_summary:
title: You may face difficulties in protecting your interests, and your ability to protect your rights through U.S. courts may be limited, because we are incorporated under Cayman Islands law. 
summary: The company is an exempted entity limited by shares under Cayman Islands law, governed by its memorandum and articles of association, the revised Companies Act, and applicable common law. Shareholder rights, director fiduciary duties, and minority shareholder actions are primarily dictated by common law, which is shaped by limited local precedents and English common law. In contrast to U.S. jurisdictions like Delaware, the Cayman Islands lacks a comprehensive framework of securities and corporate laws, resulting in ambiguity regarding shareholder rights and director responsibilities. Notably, shareholders of Cayman Islands companies do not possess general rights to inspect corporate records or access shareholder lists, as such decisions are at the discretion of the directors. This limitation may hinder shareholders' ability to gather necessary information for resolutions or proxy solicitations. Consequently, public shareholders may encounter greater difficulties in protecting their interests against management or controlling shareholders compared to their U.S. counterparts. The differences between the Cayman Islands Companies Act and U.S. corporate laws, particularly regarding shareholder rights, are further elaborated in the section titled “Description of Share Capital — Differences in Corporate Law.”
